patterns.name: Acculturation
patterns.summary: Build shared cultural understanding of why AI matters before anyone touches a tool. Addresses fear, misconceptions, and resistance at the organisational level so that skills training later sticks. Without this foundation step, formal AI training lands on soil that has not been prepared — people comply but do not change how they work. The acculturation workshop creates the shared vocabulary, cleared misconceptions, and personal motivation that all later Craft learning depends on.
patterns.intent: Create the shared cultural ground — cleared of fear and false beliefs — that makes any later AI skills training stick.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this pattern before any hands-on AI training in an organisation where staff have not yet worked with AI tools, or where prior AI rollouts met resistance. Also apply when a team has mixed attitudes toward AI and those attitudes have not been openly discussed. Do not skip it in favour of jumping straight to prompt-engineering modules.

inputs.name: Staff cohort
inputs.description: A cross-functional group of employees — technical and non-technical — who will later receive hands-on AI training but have not yet been through any structured AI introduction.

inputs.name: Facilitated session design
inputs.description: A structured workshop format (e.g. Café IA) that creates space for debate and personal reflection, not a lecture or product demo.

inputs.name: Leadership AI narrative
inputs.description: A clear statement from organisational leadership of what the company believes about AI and why it matters — communicated before the session, not after.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A participant who has named and examined their own AI assumptions, reduced anxiety, and raised genuine curiosity — ready to absorb hands-on training.

outputs.name: Shared AI vocabulary
outputs.description: A common set of terms and agreed limits for what AI can and cannot do, usable across the whole cohort from this point on.

outputs.name: Personal AI relevance statement
outputs.description: Each participant's own articulation of three specific ways AI could affect their job or industry — the Masterpiece of this foundation step.

steps.name: Surface personal mental models
steps.description: Participants share, in small groups, their current beliefs and fears about AI — without correction. The facilitator captures recurring themes on a shared board. The goal is to make hidden assumptions visible before challenging them.

steps.name: Correct with evidence, not hype
steps.description: The facilitator introduces specific evidence — real examples of what AI does and does not do well in the participants' own industry. Misconceptions identified in Step 1 are addressed directly. No product demos; the focus is on accurate mental models.

steps.name: Debate impacts in personal terms
steps.description: Participants work through structured prompts: 'What does this mean for my job in three years?' and 'What could I do with AI that I cannot do today?' Small-group discussion followed by full-group share-out. The facilitator draws out both opportunity and risk without minimising either.

steps.name: Connect to immediate skills training
steps.description: The session ends with a clear bridge: here is the hands-on training that follows this session, here is when it starts, and here is the one tool you will use first. Acculturation and skills training must be adjacent — separated sessions lose the momentum.

principles: Debate first, demo never — let participants form revised beliefs through discussion before any tool is introduced.
Fear is data — surface it openly rather than dismissing it; unspoken fear becomes resistance.
Acculturation and skills training are one movement, not two separate programmes.
patterns.threshold: The participant can name three specific ways AI affects their job or industry, can describe at least one thing AI cannot reliably do, and reports reduced anxiety and raised curiosity about hands-on practice.
patterns.masterpiece: A written personal AI relevance statement: three concrete AI impacts on the participant's own role, one stated limit of AI, and one task they want to try with AI first — produced before any tool training begins.

known_uses.name: Cafés IA (citizen debate sessions)
known_uses.org: Centre Inffo / French public training sector
known_uses.quote: la formation n'est efficace que si elle est précédée par la diffusion d'une culture générale
known_uses.note: Gilles Babinet, co-président du Conseil national du numérique, cited in 2024 Centre Inffo analysis of French AI training effectiveness.

known_uses.name: Acculturation IA Générative offering
known_uses.org: Multiple French training providers (Suricats Consulting, SavoirIA, Algos AI)
known_uses.quote: L'acculturation de l'IA est un processus de transformation culturelle et organisationnelle visant à intégrer l'intelligence artificielle dans l'ADN de l'entreprise
known_uses.note: Distinct market category in France by 2024, offered by multiple specialist providers.

known_uses.name: Tech Show Paris 2025: Formation & IA: Acculturation pour une révolution digitale réussie
known_uses.quote: Formation & IA : Acculturation pour une révolution digitale réussie
known_uses.note: Dedicated conference session in 2024 treating acculturation as a prerequisite to digital-revolution success.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of opening with a live product demo before mental models have been surfaced. Demos trigger either uncritical excitement or defensive rejection; neither produces the informed curiosity acculturation is meant to build.

The anti-pattern of running a good acculturation session and then scheduling skills training six weeks later. The gap drains the momentum; participants arrive at skills training having reverted to pre-session anxiety.

sources.title: L'acculturation, facteur clé d'efficacité des formations à l'IA
sources.quote: créer les conditions d'une appropriation collective de l'IA et de ses enjeux

sources.title: Acculturation à l'IA : le guide pour l'entreprise
sources.quote: L'acculturation de l'IA est un processus de transformation culturelle et organisationnelle

    B --> C[Correct with evidence

    C --> D[Debate impacts

Masterpiece]

patterns.example_scenario: A mid-sized French logistics company is about to roll out an AI-assisted document processing tool to 120 operations staff. The L&D lead runs three Café IA sessions before any tool access is granted. In each session, 40 staff members — warehouse supervisors, fleet coordinators, and back-office administrators — sit in groups of six and answer one question: 'What does AI mean for your job?' The facilitator captures the responses without evaluating them. Common themes emerge: fear of job loss, confusion about whether AI reads company data, and a belief that only IT staff can use it. The facilitator then introduces three short evidence pieces. The first is an anonymised transcript of an operations coordinator at a comparable company describing how she uses AI to draft carrier emails. The second is a published error rate comparison showing AI failing on ambiguous consignment notes. The third is the company's stated policy on data privacy and AI. Groups reconvene and revise their statements. In 90 minutes the tone shifts from defensive to curious. The session ends with a single sentence: 'Next Tuesday you will each spend one hour with the tool, guided by a colleague who has already used it. Your first task is yours to choose.' Anxiety scores on a five-point pre/post self-report drop from an average of 3.8 to 2.1. Every participant attends the hands-on session the following week.

patterns.name: Whole-Crew Baseline
patterns.summary: Roll out a common AI literacy baseline to every person in an organisation — not just technical staff. Everyone learns the same foundational vocabulary, responsible-use rules, and at least one AI tool, so the whole crew can participate in AI change. Without a shared baseline, AI adoption splits organisations into a capable minority and an excluded majority. The baseline closes that gap by treating AI literacy as a universal operating requirement, the same way reading and email literacy became universal in prior decades.
patterns.intent: Give every person in the organisation the same minimum AI vocabulary, responsible-use awareness, and at least one proven hands-on skill.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when an organisation is beginning a meaningful AI adoption programme and needs every employee, not just early adopters, to reach a common starting point. Also apply when a new regulatory obligation (such as EU AI Act Article 4) requires documented literacy for all staff who use AI in their work.

inputs.name: Defined literacy bar
inputs.description: A written statement of exactly what every employee must be able to do and know: use a named AI tool, spot a hallucination, state the responsible-use policy, and report a misuse. This bar must be set before the module is built.

inputs.name: Self-paced module with role-specific examples
inputs.description: A short course — typically three to eight hours — containing worked examples drawn from real roles in the organisation, not generic AI demonstrations.

inputs.name: Responsible-use policy
inputs.description: A published document stating what employees may and may not do with AI tools at work. The baseline module and the policy must land together; policy without training or training without policy both fail.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: An employee who can use at least one AI tool on a real work task, articulate the limits of AI output, and name the responsible-use rules that apply to their role.

outputs.name: Organisation-wide completion record
outputs.description: A documented completion rate across all departments — the Masterpiece — that demonstrates the baseline has actually landed, not merely been made available.

outputs.name: Responsible-use acknowledgement
outputs.description: A timestamped, per-employee record confirming they have read and understood the responsible-use policy alongside the baseline module.

steps.name: Set the minimum literacy bar
steps.description: Define, in writing, what every employee must be able to do after the baseline: name and use one AI tool, detect a hallucination, state the data-privacy rule, and know how to report a concern. This bar drives all content decisions and is the test for completion.

steps.name: Build role-specific worked examples
steps.description: For each major role cluster in the organisation, create at least one worked example that shows the AI tool solving a task the employee actually faces — not a generic demonstration. Examples are the primary reason baseline modules succeed or fail.

steps.name: Pair with responsible-use policy and gate access
steps.description: Require completion of the module and acknowledgement of the policy before granting access to the organisation's AI tools. This pairing ensures the rules and the skills land at the same moment.

steps.name: Track completion, not scores
steps.description: Measure the organisation's health by completion rate across all departments — the goal is 80% or more within six months. Use department completion rates, not individual scores, as the management metric and the evidence for regulatory compliance.

principles: Universal means everyone, not just the willing — completion is the metric, not enrolment.
Role-specific examples outperform generic demos; if the example does not look like the learner's job, the learning does not transfer.
Policy and training are one delivery, not two — they must arrive together.
patterns.threshold: At least 80% of all employees in scope have completed the module, acknowledged the responsible-use policy, and can demonstrate at least one AI-assisted work task from their own role.
patterns.masterpiece: A documented organisation-wide completion record showing the baseline literacy bar reached across all departments, with a responsible-use acknowledgement on file for every completing employee — produceable as evidence for a regulatory audit.

known_uses.name: IKEA AI literacy rollout
known_uses.quote: The programme offers a range of learning materials for understanding and using AI
known_uses.note: 40,000 employees trained through August 2024 (out of ~165,000); includes responsible AI and ethics training.

known_uses.name: JPMorgan Chase prompt engineering baseline
known_uses.quote: This year, everyone coming in here will have prompt engineering training to get them ready for the AI of the future
known_uses.note: CEO mandate: all incoming staff receive prompt engineering as a baseline literacy move.

known_uses.name: MasterCard responsible-AI hub
known_uses.quote: Eight hours of video training on responsible AI principles (fairness, transparency)
known_uses.note: Delivered via a new company intranet hub, launched August 2024.

known_uses.name: Elements of AI — EU 1% citizen goal
known_uses.quote: train one per cent of EU citizens in the basics of artificial intelligence
known_uses.note: Over 2 million enrolled across 170+ countries in 40+ languages; the canonical national-scale version of this move.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of reporting enrolment numbers as if they were completion numbers. A baseline that 90% of staff are enrolled in but only 40% finish has not landed; it has been opened.
The anti-pattern of using an off-the-shelf AI literacy course whose examples do not match the organisation's tools or roles. Completion rates stay low and transfer to real work stays near zero.

sources.title: These companies are training all their staff on AI, here's why
sources.quote: Large companies such as MasterCard, JPMorgan Chase and S&P Global are rolling out programmes to help prepare employees across the organisation for the AI era, not just technical staff

sources.title: Elements of AI — A free online introduction to artificial intelligence for non-experts
sources.quote: AI is going to have as big an impact on our society as electricity

sources.title: EU AI Act Article 4: AI literacy obligation
sources.quote: Providers and deployers of AI systems shall take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf

patterns.example_scenario: A European financial services firm with 3,200 employees must comply with EU AI Act Article 4 by mid-2025. The L&D director commissions an internal baseline module. She starts by setting the bar in writing: every employee must be able to open the company's licensed AI assistant, complete a task from a provided list, and sign the acceptable-use policy. The content team builds five role clusters — client-facing staff, operations, finance, IT, and management — and writes one worked example per cluster using tasks from the firm's own workflows. The module takes four hours per cluster variant. All five variants are published simultaneously with the acceptable-use policy, and tool access is gated behind completion. A dashboard shows per-department completion rates updated daily. At week four, the back-office operations cluster is at 62%; the department head sends a team message asking all outstanding staff to complete by Friday. By week twelve, overall completion is 84%. The firm's compliance team exports the completion records and policy acknowledgements as the evidence package for its Article 4 documentation. Six months later a random sample of twenty completers is tested on the literacy bar items — seventeen pass without re-training.

patterns.name: AI-as-Mentor
patterns.summary: The AI tool itself coaches the learner: it asks Socratic questions, gives context-sensitive feedback, and never just hands over the answer. Learning and doing happen in the same environment, removing the gap between training and work. This matters because most AI training creates a step between learning and use — the learner studies a course, then returns to their real task with only memory to guide them. The AI-as-Mentor move collapses that gap: the learner brings a real problem to the AI, and the AI teaches them how to work with it on that exact problem.
patterns.intent: Let the AI tool teach the learner how to use it, on the learner's own real problems, without switching to a separate training environment.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when a learner has a real work problem in front of them and needs both to solve it and to build lasting AI skills. It is most effective after basic acculturation has removed the initial fear barrier. Do not apply it as the first exposure to AI for an anxious learner — a small amount of structured introduction first produces better outcomes than a purely guided discovery start.

inputs.name: Learner with a real work problem
inputs.description: A person who has an actual task — a document to draft, a data set to summarise, a decision to frame — that they want to complete, not a synthetic exercise invented for training purposes.

inputs.name: AI tool configured to guide rather than solve
inputs.description: The same AI tool the learner will use in daily work, set up (via system prompt, course scaffolding, or mode selection) to ask questions and offer hints rather than produce complete answers immediately.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A person who has solved a real task using the AI and who can articulate which prompting choices they made and why — able to replicate and adapt the approach on the next task without human instruction.

outputs.name: Solved real task
outputs.description: The completed work item the learner brought to the session — the Masterpiece — produced through their own guided iteration rather than AI-authored in one step.

steps.name: Bring a real problem
steps.description: The learner identifies a task from their actual work — not a training exercise — and opens the AI tool. They describe the task in their own words to the AI.

steps.name: Let the AI ask first
steps.description: The AI (configured for Socratic guidance) responds with a clarifying question or a prompt scaffold rather than a complete answer. The learner responds to the question and sees how the AI's output improves with more precise input.

steps.name: Iterate and note what changed
steps.description: The learner refines their request based on the AI's output. After two or three rounds, they pause and write one sentence about what they did that produced a better result. This reflection is the core learning act.

steps.name: Complete the task and review
steps.description: The learner produces the final output using the AI's help, then reviews it critically: is it accurate, is it usable, what would they need to check? The Discernment step is always explicit, never assumed.

principles: The tool teaches; the human learns by doing, not watching — no passive lesson mode.
Real problems transfer; synthetic exercises do not — the learner's own work context is the curriculum.
Reflection is not optional — the one sentence written after each session is what turns task completion into skill.
patterns.threshold: The learner completes a full real task end-to-end using only AI assistance (no human tutor), and can explain in one paragraph which prompting choices led to the best output and what they would check before using the result at work.
patterns.masterpiece: A finished, real work output — a document, an analysis, a plan — produced by the learner through guided AI iteration, accompanied by a written reflection of at least three prompting lessons learned during the session.

known_uses.name: OpenAI AI Foundations — ChatGPT as tutor and practice space
known_uses.quote: A full learning experience is available directly inside ChatGPT, where learners can practice real tasks, receive feedback in context, and reflect on their work in a single environment
known_uses.note: AI Foundations course launched 2025/2026; ChatGPT acts as tutor, practice space, and feedback loop simultaneously.

known_uses.name: Khanmigo (Khan Academy AI tutor)
known_uses.quote: Unlike other AI tools such as ChatGPT, Khanmigo doesn't just give answers. Instead, with limitless patience, it guides learners to find the answer themselves.
known_uses.note: Piloted in 260+ US school districts; available free to teachers in 40+ countries via Microsoft partnership.

known_uses.name: Anthropic AI Fluency course — Claude as learning partner
known_uses.quote: This course has something for everyone, whether you're new to Claude or a seasoned AI practitioner.
known_uses.note: Free structured course; learners use Claude while taking the course about Claude — AI-as-mentor pattern embedded by design.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the learner rephrasing their question until the AI gives a complete answer, then copying it without understanding. The session produces an output but no skill. The reflection step is the only reliable counter.
Configuring the AI to withhold answers so aggressively that the learner becomes frustrated and gives up before the task is complete. The Socratic mode must be calibrated — hints, not silence.

patterns.example_scenario: A marketing coordinator at a retail company has to write a monthly competitive summary report. She has heard of AI but has never used it for real work. Her manager suggests she try using the company's licensed AI assistant for this month's report instead of searching and writing manually. She opens the tool and types: 'Write me a competitive summary for our Q2 report.' The AI (configured with a Socratic system prompt) responds: 'That sounds useful. Which competitors do you want me to cover, and what does your audience care most about — pricing, product features, or market positioning?' She realises she had not thought about that. She types: 'Three competitors. The audience is our sales team, who care about price and product gaps.' The AI returns a structured outline with placeholders for the three competitors. It adds a prompt asking whether she can give the price points for each competitor, or whether it should note where she would need to fill those in. She fills in what she knows and the AI produces a first draft. She reads it critically, spots one claim that does not match what she knows from a recent trade show, and asks the AI to revise that section. The final report takes 40 minutes instead of her usual three hours. She writes in her reflection: 'I learned that naming my audience changes the whole structure. I should always tell the AI who will read the output before I start.'

patterns.name: Home-Forged Training
patterns.summary: Training is built by your own subject-matter experts, not bought off the shelf. Because the examples, language, and tools match the learner's actual job, the content earns more trust and sees higher completion and retention. Off-the-shelf AI courses from vendors are generalist by necessity. They cannot reference your internal systems, your real data shapes, your tool configuration, or the actual mistakes your staff make. Home-forged courses do all of these things, and that specificity is what converts training attendance into changed work behaviour.
patterns.intent: Produce AI training that learners trust because it uses their own tools, their own examples, and their colleagues as authors.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when an organisation has identified internal staff who already use AI well and wants to scale those capabilities to colleagues. It is the right choice when generic vendor content has failed to produce behaviour change, when the organisation has unusual tools or workflows, or when the responsible-use rules are company-specific and cannot be delegated to an external provider to explain.

inputs.name: Internal AI champions
inputs.description: Employees who already use AI tools effectively in their own roles and are willing to author or co-author course material. These are the credibility anchor of home-forged content.

inputs.name: Real company workflows and data shapes
inputs.description: The actual tasks, documents, systems, and data formats that employees encounter — used as the worked examples in the course instead of generic or simulated scenarios.

inputs.name: Internal tool configuration
inputs.description: The specific AI tools the organisation has licensed, including any custom settings, approved use cases, and access restrictions — content must match what employees can actually do on their machines.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: An employee who has completed training built from their own work context and who can apply AI to their real tasks immediately, with no translation layer between training and job.

outputs.name: High-completion internal course
outputs.description: A course with verifiable high completion — 90% or more of enrolled staff — because the content is seen as directly relevant rather than generic. This is the Masterpiece of the Home-Forged move.

outputs.name: Internal AI qualification
outputs.description: A company-specific credential, tied to passing the course, that signals AI readiness within the organisation for role progression or tool access.

steps.name: Identify and recruit internal champions
steps.description: Find the colleagues who already use AI well — they are usually visible through their output quality or through word of mouth. Invite them to author worked examples for their own role cluster. The authoring process itself deepens their mastery and gives them social credibility within the organisation.

steps.name: Build from real workflows
steps.description: Each champion drafts two or three worked examples using actual tasks from their week — not invented scenarios. Examples show a before state (the task done the old way) and an after state (the same task with AI), including the prompt they used and what they checked before using the output.

steps.name: Gate with an internal qualification
steps.description: Set a completion gate: passing the course earns a named internal credential (e.g. 'AI Operator — Level 1'). Tie the credential to a concrete benefit: access to the organisation's advanced AI tools, eligibility for the next training track, or inclusion in the AI Champions network. The credential makes completion desirable rather than merely required.

steps.name: Iterate quarterly
steps.description: AI tools and policies change fast. Schedule a quarterly review where the original champions update worked examples to reflect current tool versions and add examples from new use cases that have emerged. Internal authors can respond to tool updates within days; a vendor course cannot.

principles: Trust comes from recognition — learners complete training they can see themselves in; generic examples are skipped.
Champions are authors, not endorsers — their hands-on involvement in writing the course is what makes it credible, not their name on a banner.
Internal qualification changes the incentive from compliance to aspiration.
patterns.threshold: 90% or more of enrolled employees complete the course, and graduates can demonstrate the target AI skill on an actual internal task — not on the training exercise — within two weeks of completion.
patterns.masterpiece: A live internal AI course, authored by employees, with 90%+ completion, a named internal qualification, and documented quarterly update cadence — showing that AI capability is spreading peer-to-peer, not only top-down.

known_uses.name: CyberAgent ChatGPT Enterprise 101 and custom internal workshop series
known_uses.quote: more than 100 employees participating in each of over ten sessions that ranged from beginner-friendly introductions such as 'ChatGPT Enterprise 101' to workshops on custom GPTs, hands-on Codex sessions, and internal hackathons
known_uses.note: ChatGPT Enterprise reached 93% monthly active user rate. Sessions were hosted by OpenAI but delivered as internal programming; the home-forged pattern is visible in the internally-customised content tracks.

known_uses.name: Anthropic AI Fluency CC-licensed framework for internal adaptation
known_uses.quote: HR and L&D leaders are implementing this framework by… adapting the Creative Commons–licensed content for internal programs
known_uses.note: Anthropic explicitly licenses the 4D framework for organisations to build their own home-forged courses on top of it.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of relying on one or two internal champions to author all content. When those people change roles or leave, the course stops being updated and quickly becomes stale — worse than a vendor course because at least vendor courses are maintained.

Creating an internal AI credential that has no concrete benefit attached to it. If passing the course changes nothing about what the employee can access or do, completion rates drop toward the level of compliance training.

sources.title: CyberAgent moves faster with ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex
sources.quote: ChatGPT Enterprise reached a monthly active user rate of 93% at CyberAgent

sources.title: Anthropic's AI Fluency Course: How to Upskill Your Org with the 4D Framework
sources.quote: adapting the Creative Commons–licensed content for internal programs

patterns.example_scenario: A 600-person insurance broker has licensed an AI assistant for all staff but usage after two months is under 20%. The HR director asks five well-known AI enthusiasts across the firm to each write two worked examples from their last month of real work. The five are a claims handler, a broker, a finance analyst, a compliance officer, and an operations coordinator. The claims handler writes one example showing how she uses the AI to draft a first response to a complex claim, including her exact prompt and the one thing she always edits before sending. The compliance officer writes an example of using the AI to flag whether a proposed policy wording conflicts with a known regulatory rule, and explains the check she runs to verify the AI's reading. All five examples are assembled into a four-hour course alongside the firm's acceptable-use policy. The course earns the internal credential 'AI Operator — Claims / Broker / Finance / Compliance / Operations' depending on which track is completed. Completion of the credential gates access to the firm's advanced AI modules launching in Q3. Within six weeks, completion is at 88%. A quarterly review three months later: the claims handler updates her example because the AI assistant has a new document upload feature that changes her workflow. The update takes her 90 minutes. A vendor-maintained course would have taken three months to reflect the same change.

patterns.name: Regulatory Literacy Mandate
patterns.summary: A legal or regulatory obligation forces organisations to establish a minimum AI literacy baseline for all staff who work with AI systems. The mandate turns literacy from optional to mandatory and creates documentation pressure that drives real training investment. EU AI Act Article 4, in force since February 2025 with no grace period, is the primary current instance. It requires providers and deployers to ensure a sufficient level of AI literacy for all staff who deal with AI systems, taking into account their technical knowledge and the specific AI systems they use. The mandate's force comes from its documentation requirement: organisations must be able to show what training they provided, to whom, and how it was tailored to risk context.
patterns.intent: Meet a legal AI literacy obligation with training that satisfies the documentation standard and is specific enough to the roles and systems in scope to hold up under audit.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this pattern when an organisation operates in a jurisdiction that has enacted an AI literacy requirement (currently the EU under AI Act Article 4) and deploys AI systems in professional contexts. It is also a useful forcing function in organisations where training investment would not otherwise be approved — the legal requirement converts a discretionary budget line into a compliance obligation.

inputs.name: Role-to-AI-system mapping
inputs.description: A documented list of every staff role that touches an AI system, paired with which AI system they use and what the primary risk context of that use is. This mapping is required to design role-specific training rather than generic content.

inputs.name: Role-specific training tracks
inputs.description: Separate training content for each role cluster, covering the specific AI tools and risk contexts those employees encounter — not a single generic course applied to all staff.

inputs.name: Completion and acknowledgement records
inputs.description: A system to document who was trained, when, on what content, and with what evidence of comprehension. The obligation is best-efforts but evidence is required.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: An employee who understands the AI tools they use in their role, the specific risks those tools carry in their context, and how to escalate a concern — capable of using AI responsibly in their daily work.

outputs.name: Audit-ready training documentation
outputs.description: A package of role-specific training records, policy acknowledgements, and review-cycle documentation that demonstrates compliance with Article 4 or equivalent regulation — the Masterpiece of this pattern.

steps.name: Map roles to AI systems and risk contexts
steps.description: Identify every staff member who uses an AI system in their professional work. For each role, record: which AI system they use, what they use it for, and what the primary risk if the AI produces a wrong output is. This map drives everything else.

steps.name: Design role-specific training tracks
steps.description: Build separate training content for each role cluster. Generic training has been identified in regulatory guidance as insufficient. Each track must cover: what the AI system does, what it cannot do, the specific risks in that role's context, and how to report a problem.

steps.name: Document completion and acknowledgement
steps.description: Record completion per employee with a timestamp. Require explicit acknowledgement — not just completion — that the employee has understood the policy and the role-specific risks. Store records in a system retrievable for audit.

steps.name: Review and update annually or on system change
steps.description: The obligation does not have a fixed content standard that can be met once. When an AI system is upgraded, a new use case is adopted, or a year has passed, the relevant training tracks must be reviewed and updated. Outdated training is not compliant training.

principles: Role-specific always outperforms generic — the law requires it and completion rates confirm it.
Documentation is part of the training programme, not an afterthought — without records, the training did not happen in regulatory terms.
Annual review is the minimum; system changes trigger immediate review.
patterns.threshold: Every in-scope employee has a timestamped, role-specific training record and policy acknowledgement on file, and the training content references the specific AI systems and risk context of their role.
patterns.masterpiece: An audit-ready compliance package: a role-to-AI-system mapping, role-specific training records for all in-scope staff, policy acknowledgements, and a documented annual review cycle — demonstrating best-efforts compliance with Article 4 or equivalent.

known_uses.name: EU AI Act Article 4 — AI literacy obligation
known_uses.quote: Providers and deployers of AI systems shall take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf
known_uses.note: Entered into application 2 February 2025. No grace period. Applies to any European employer whose staff uses AI tools professionally.

known_uses.name: AI Literacy Programs in Europe — supporting Article 4
known_uses.quote: Generic or non-role-specific training has been identified in regulatory guidance as insufficient to meet the legal standard.
known_uses.note: Catalogues EU-recognised literacy programs that satisfy the Article 4 standard.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of applying a single off-the-shelf AI literacy course to all staff and calling the obligation met. Regulatory guidance explicitly identifies generic training as insufficient; this approach produces documentation that does not hold up under scrutiny.

Recording that training was completed without documenting that it was role-specific and risk-aware. Completion records for generic content do not demonstrate that the person understands the AI systems they personally use and the risks in their specific context.

sources.title: Article 4: AI literacy — EU Artificial Intelligence Act
sources.quote: Providers and deployers of AI systems shall take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff

sources.title: EU AI Act Article 4: AI Literacy Obligation for Providers and Deployers (2026)
sources.quote: The obligation applies now. The European Commission has confirmed there is no grace period

sources.title: AI literacy is now a legal duty: Article 4 of the EU AI Act, in plain language
sources.quote: A deployer is anyone using an AI system in a professional capacity — which is the box almost every European employer sits in the moment a member of staff opens ChatGPT

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Organisation uses
AI systems professionally] --> B[Map roles to
AI systems
+ risk contexts]
    B --> C[Design role-specific
training tracks
not generic]
    C --> D[Deliver training
per role cluster]
    D --> E[Record completion
+ acknowledgement
with timestamp]
    E --> F{All in-scope
staff covered?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Audit-ready
compliance package
Masterpiece]
    G --> H[Annual review
or on system change]
    H --> B

patterns.example_scenario: A Dutch HR-tech company with 85 employees integrates an AI-powered CV screening tool into its recruitment service in March 2025. The compliance lead realises the firm is a deployer under the EU AI Act and Article 4 is already in force. She maps the roles in scope: three recruiters who use the tool daily, two account managers who review AI shortlists with clients, and the engineering team who configured the tool. She identifies four distinct risk contexts. Recruiters face the risk of the AI misreading a candidate's qualifications. Account managers face the risk of presenting an AI-generated shortlist as if it were human-reviewed. Engineers face the risk of misconfiguring the scoring model. She builds three short training tracks — one per role cluster — each covering what the specific tool does, what it cannot do reliably, and what to check before using its output. The recruiter track includes a worked example of a CV the tool scored incorrectly and how the recruiter caught it. Completion is recorded in the HR system with acknowledgement of the acceptable-use policy. The firm's legal counsel exports the records, the role-to-system mapping, and the training content as the Article 4 evidence package. When the CV screening tool is upgraded six months later with a new bias-detection module, the recruiter training track is updated within two weeks to reflect the change.

patterns.name: Vendor Cert Ladder
patterns.summary: A learner follows a structured, vendor-provided certification ladder from zero AI knowledge to a credentialled, verifiable proof of literacy. The external credential signals readiness to employers and creates a shared vocabulary within teams. The six major AI platforms — Google, Microsoft, AWS, IBM, Anthropic, OpenAI — each offer a foundational certificate requiring no technical prerequisites. These certs range from free (IBM, Anthropic) to under $150 (AWS, Microsoft), take two to eight hours of self-paced work, and produce a verifiable badge. The Vendor Cert Ladder move treats these as stackable: the learner starts with one, earns the badge, and can advance to deeper tracks on the same platform or cross to another platform's offering.
patterns.intent: Give an individual learner a structured, externally credentialled path from zero AI knowledge to a verifiable proof of operator-level literacy.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when an individual learner wants structured guidance rather than self-directed exploration, or needs an externally verifiable credential for employment or progression. Also apply it when an organisation wants a vendor-neutral way to set a literacy bar without building their own course. Also apply when EU AI Act Article 4 documentation is required and an organisation chooses recognised vendor certs as the evidence base.

inputs.name: Learner at zero or low AI literacy
inputs.description: An individual who has little or no prior AI experience and wants structured learning toward a credential — no technical background required for foundational tier certs.

inputs.name: Selected foundational cert
inputs.description: One cert from the ecosystem the learner works in or aspires to: Google AI Essentials, Microsoft AI-901, AWS AIF-C01, IBM AI Literacy, Anthropic AI Fluency, or OpenAI AI Foundations — each appropriate for non-technical learners.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A person who has completed structured AI literacy training and can use the certified platform's tools on real tasks, evaluate AI outputs critically, and apply responsible-use principles in their work.

outputs.name: Verifiable digital badge or certificate
outputs.description: An externally issued, publicly linkable credential tied to the learner's identity — the Masterpiece — demonstrating that a defined literacy standard has been met and providing evidence for employers or regulatory compliance.

steps.name: Select the right cert for the ecosystem
steps.description: Match the cert to where the learner will use AI. Pick Google AI Essentials for Workspace users, Microsoft AI-901 for M365 or Azure environments, and AWS AIF-C01 for cloud-first businesses. Pick IBM SkillsBuild for platform-neutral literacy, Anthropic AI Fluency for Claude users, and OpenAI AI Foundations for ChatGPT users. The worked examples in each cert are platform-specific; the right choice produces higher relevance and lower study time.

steps.name: Complete self-paced modules
steps.description: Work through each module in the selected cert. All foundational certs cover: what AI and generative AI are, how to prompt effectively, how to evaluate output quality, and how to use AI responsibly. Target two to eight hours depending on the cert chosen.

steps.name: Pass the assessment and earn the badge
steps.description: Complete the proctored or auto-graded assessment. For AWS and Microsoft, this is a separate exam booking. For Google, IBM, Anthropic, and OpenAI, assessment is embedded in the course. Pass and claim the verifiable digital badge via Credly, Google Certificates, or equivalent.

steps.name: Apply the certified skills on a real task
steps.description: Within a week of earning the badge, complete one real work task using the AI tools covered in the cert. The badge is evidence of literacy; the real task is the proof of transfer. If no real task emerges, the cert was the wrong one for the learner's job context.

principles: Match cert to ecosystem — a well-chosen cert takes half the study time of a mis-matched one and transfers twice as well.
The badge is evidence of literacy, not proof of skill — the real task after the cert is what closes the loop.
Stack from foundational to specialised — the Vendor Cert Ladder is a path, not a single step.
patterns.threshold: The learner holds a verifiable badge from a recognised AI foundational certification and can apply at least one technique from the cert to a real task in their current role within one week of completing the assessment.
patterns.masterpiece: A verifiable, publicly-linkable digital badge or certificate from a recognised AI provider, earned through assessment, tied to the learner's professional identity — demonstrating to employers, clients, or regulators that a defined AI literacy standard has been met.

known_uses.name: Google AI Essentials
known_uses.quote: 67% of workers who used GenAI estimated using AI saves them 2+ hours per week
known_uses.note: 5 modules, <5 hours, no technical prerequisites, earns a Google Certificate. Available on Coursera for ~$49/month.

known_uses.name: Microsoft Azure AI Fundamentals (AI-900 / AI-901)
known_uses.quote: Both certifications are designed for candidates from technical and non-technical backgrounds—prior experience in data science or software engineering is not required
known_uses.note: AI-900 retiring June 2026, replaced by AI-901. Available in 13 languages. ACE college credit eligible.

known_uses.name: AWS Certified AI Practitioner (AIF-C01)
known_uses.quote: The entire exam content outline for AWS Certified AI Practitioner focuses on AI, ML, and generative AI
known_uses.note: Launched October 2024. 90-minute exam, $100, 12 languages. Target: business analysts, marketers, project managers. 47% salary uplift cited for AI-skilled IT professionals.

known_uses.name: Anthropic AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations
known_uses.quote: This course has something for everyone, whether you're new to Claude or a seasoned AI practitioner.
known_uses.note: Free. Designed with Prof. Joseph Feller (UCC) and Prof. Rick Dakan (Ringling College). Teaches the 4D framework: Delegation, Description, Discernment, Diligence.

known_uses.name: IBM SkillsBuild AI Literacy
known_uses.quote: recognize, evaluate, and use AI responsibly
known_uses.note: Free, 4 hours, earns an IBM Digital Badge via Credly. Open to students, job seekers, educators, and lifelong learners.

known_uses.name: OpenAI AI Foundations (OpenAI Academy)
known_uses.quote: OpenAI is accelerating its push to certify 10 million Americans by 2030
known_uses.note: Delivered inside ChatGPT. Piloted 2025–2026 with Walmart, John Deere, BCG, Hearst. Uses ETS and Credly for psychometric rigour.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of earning a cert and never applying the skills to a real task. The badge is not the goal; changed work behaviour is. A learner who completes a cert and returns to working exactly as before has not advanced on the Craft Path.

Choosing a cert for the wrong ecosystem — for example, completing the AWS AI Practitioner when the organisation uses Google Workspace. The worked examples do not transfer and the learner finishes knowing a platform they never use.

sources.title: Google AI Essentials
sources.quote: for anyone who wants to use AI to help save time and work more effectively

sources.title: Microsoft Certified: Azure AI Fundamentals
sources.quote: designed for candidates from technical and non-technical backgrounds

sources.title: AWS Certified AI Practitioner (AIF-C01)
sources.quote: The entire exam content outline for AWS Certified AI Practitioner focuses on AI, ML, and generative AI

sources.title: AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations — Anthropic
sources.quote: I want to prepare people for the long term. Not just how to use our latest features, but how to think about and work with AI

sources.title: IBM SkillsBuild AI Literacy
sources.quote: students, job seekers, educators, or lifelong learners

sources.title: Launching our first OpenAI Certifications courses
sources.quote: A full learning experience is available directly inside ChatGPT, where learners can practice real tasks, receive feedback in context

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Learner at zero
or low AI literacy] --> B[Select cert
matched to ecosystem
Google / MS / AWS / IBM / Anthropic / OpenAI]
    B --> C[Complete self-paced

patterns.example_scenario: A project manager at a mid-sized construction firm has been told by her director that the company will start using AI tools for site report summarisation and tender drafting within the next quarter. She has no prior AI experience and feels behind. She looks at the cert options and picks Google AI Essentials because the firm already uses Google Workspace. She completes the five modules over four evenings — about four and a half hours total. The third module on prompting is the most useful; she tries the techniques directly in her Workspace while working through it. She passes the final assessment on the first attempt and her Coursera certificate appears the same day. The following Monday she drafts the summary section of a site inspection report using Google's AI tool with a prompt she adapted from the module's worked example. It takes twelve minutes instead of her usual 45. She clips the prompt and the output and sends them to her director as evidence that the cert was worth the time. Her director asks her to run a lunch session for the other three project managers. She adds the Google Certificate to her LinkedIn profile that evening. The badge is public and verifiable. Two weeks later she receives a recruiter message referencing it.

patterns.name: National-Scale Literacy Drive
patterns.summary: A government or national body funds and deploys a free, multi-language AI literacy programme targeting citizens or the whole workforce, not just tech professionals. Scale is the goal; the programme sets a shared national baseline and unlocks deeper workforce tracks. The canonical instances are Elements of AI (Finland/EU), One Million Prompters (Dubai), SkillsFuture AI (Singapore), and Japan's MEXT Literacy Level programme. They share three properties. They are free, they carry a measurable scale target (1% of EU citizens, 1 million individuals, a national population), and they are designed for people with no prior technical background. These programmes set the floor of public AI literacy in their jurisdictions and create the conditions for employer-level training to build on.
patterns.intent: Set a shared national AI literacy floor by deploying a free, multi-language programme at population scale, giving every citizen a minimum vocabulary and a first hands-on AI experience.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this track when a government or national body wants to close the AI literacy gap across the whole workforce, not just among technology workers. It is also the right framing when a national programme already exists but learner uptake is low — the track describes both how to design and how to drive adoption of a national programme.

inputs.name: Government or national sponsor
inputs.description: A ministry, agency, or national foundation with a mandate and budget to reach citizens at scale — not a commercial provider, though university partners are common co-authors of the content.

inputs.name: University or expert consortium
inputs.description: The content authors: typically a university AI faculty working under a national commission, as with the University of Helsinki for Elements of AI or MEXT-commissioned universities in Japan.

inputs.name: National distribution channels
inputs.description: The channels that can reach non-technical citizens: national skills platforms, employer networks, schools, public broadcasters, and employer mandates or credits that incentivise completion.

inputs.name: Measurable scale target
inputs.description: A specific, public enrolment or completion goal — 1% of EU citizens, 1 million individuals in three years — that anchors the programme's ambition and creates political accountability for delivery.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A citizen who can describe what AI is and is not, recognise AI-generated content, use at least one AI tool to complete a task, and identify a responsible-use concern when they encounter one.

outputs.name: National literacy baseline reached
outputs.description: A documented enrolment or completion milestone — the Masterpiece — demonstrating that a defined proportion of the target population has completed the programme and can be treated as AI-literate in workforce and policy planning.

outputs.name: Completion certificate recognised by employers
outputs.description: A credential that employers in the jurisdiction recognise as evidence of a minimum AI literacy standard — enabling the national programme to function as a feeder into deeper organisational training.

steps.name: Commission content from a trusted academic or expert body
steps.description: Fund a university or expert consortium to design the course content. Academic authorship provides credibility and political neutrality that government-authored content often lacks. The content must be open-licensed so it can be translated and adapted by regional partners.

steps.name: Set a public, measurable scale target
steps.description: Announce a specific target — not 'as many as possible' but a defined number or percentage. The target creates urgency, attracts media coverage, and gives programme operators a clear gate to work toward. Finland's 1% goal and Dubai's 1 million prompters target are both examples of this.

steps.name: Deploy through multiple national channels simultaneously
steps.description: Make the programme available through every channel at once: national skills platforms, schools, employers, and digital advertising. Incentivise completion through existing national mechanisms — SkillsFuture credits in Singapore, SkillsFuture recognition with employers in Finland. Free access is table stakes; active distribution is the work.

steps.name: Refresh content and add sectoral modules
steps.description: AI capabilities change quickly. The base programme must be reviewed at least annually and sectoral modules added for high-priority industries. The base remains free; sectoral modules may be subsidised or funded by industry bodies.

principles: Free is the minimum — a cost barrier, however small, excludes the people the programme most needs to reach.
A named scale target is not vanity — it changes how the programme is resourced, distributed, and measured.
Employer recognition makes completion worth something; without it, the certificate is only personal.
patterns.threshold: The programme reaches its stated enrolment milestone, and completion certificate holders are recognised by at least a defined set of major employers in the jurisdiction as meeting a minimum AI literacy standard for employment or advancement.
patterns.masterpiece: A documented national enrolment milestone — for example, one million completions — with a sample of employer recognitions confirming that the programme credential is being used in hiring or progression decisions.

known_uses.name: Elements of AI (Finland / EU)
known_uses.quote: AI is going to have as big an impact on our society as electricity
known_uses.note: 2 million+ learners, 170+ countries, 40+ languages. Finland's EU Presidency 2019 gift: offer to all Member States for free with goal to train 1% of EU citizens.

known_uses.name: Singapore SkillsFuture AI programmes
known_uses.quote: 97% increase in employer demand for the 'AI Application' skill
known_uses.note: State-funded, subsidised up to 70%, 1784+ AI courses on the portal as of 2024. Workers receive SkillsFuture credits to spend on approved courses.

known_uses.name: One Million Prompters (Dubai / UAE)
known_uses.org: Dubai Future Foundation / Dubai Centre for AI
known_uses.quote: We want to be the most future-ready city and continue preparing for the AI era by developing expertise and skills that support global technological transformation
known_uses.note: Launched May 2024. 4 modules × 1 hour. Target: 1 million individuals trained in AI and prompt literacy over 3 years. Arabic-language delivery.

known_uses.name: Japan MEXT Mathematics, Data Science and AI Education Programme (Literacy Level)
known_uses.quote: all humanities and science students of universities and colleges of technology should master introductory-level mathematics, data science and AI
known_uses.note: 382 certified Literacy-Level programmes across Japanese universities as of Aug 2023. Co-administered by MEXT, Cabinet Office, and METI.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of announcing a large scale goal but relying on passive discovery — learners finding the programme on their own. Without active distribution through employer networks, schools, and national platforms, enrolment stalls at the already-motivated audience and never reaches the people the programme is designed for.

Issuing a completion certificate that no employer or institution recognises. If the certificate has no value in hiring or progression, completion rates remain low among working adults who need a practical return on their time.

sources.title: Elements of AI — join the movement!
sources.quote: train one per cent of EU citizens in the basics of artificial intelligence

sources.title: Elements of AI online course now available in 22 EU countries and languages
sources.quote: Over 2 million students have signed up for the Elements of AI course, with graduating students from over 170 countries

sources.title: One Million Prompters — Dubai Centre for AI
sources.quote: equip one million individuals with critical AI and prompt literacy skills

sources.quote: 数理・データサイエンス・AI教育プログラム（リテラシーレベル）

sources.title: SkillsFuture AI Course Singapore
sources.quote: practical, job-ready skills with little to no upfront cost, with subsidies available for working adults and mid-career professionals

flowchart TD

national body
with literacy mandate] --> B[Commission content
from university

    B --> C[Set public

    C --> D[Open-license and translate

modules]

patterns.example_scenario: Finland holds the EU Council Presidency in the second half of 2019 and decides to make AI literacy its signature digital gift to Europe. The government partners with the University of Helsinki and MinnaLearn, who have already built a six-chapter online AI course in Finnish. The programme team adds English and Swedish translations, makes the course free to anyone with an internet connection, and announces a single public goal: train 1% of EU citizens. Finland's national broadcaster and its largest employers share the course link. Within six months, over 100,000 Finns complete it — about 2% of the population. European partners localise the course into 22 languages with funding from their own governments. By 2024, over 2 million people from 170 countries have enrolled. Finnish employers begin listing Elements of AI completion as a preferred qualification for office roles. The course earns a Webby Award. The team adds a more advanced 'Building AI' module for learners who want to go beyond literacy. The masterpiece is documented: over 2 million completions, employer recognition in multiple countries, and a national AI workforce that EU researchers cite as a benchmark for digital-era public education.

patterns.name: 4D Fluency Framework
patterns.summary: A structured four-part mental model — Delegation, Description, Discernment, Diligence — that gives learners a lasting cognitive scaffold for working with AI, not just instructions for a specific tool. The framework is designed to stay useful as models change. Most AI literacy training teaches what buttons to press. The 4D framework instead teaches four questions to ask. What can I delegate to AI? How do I describe what I want clearly? How do I evaluate whether the output is good, and how do I use AI safely and ethically? Developed by Anthropic with professors from University College Cork and Ringling College, the framework is published under a Creative Commons licence so organisations can build home-forged courses on top of it.
patterns.intent: Give the learner a four-part cognitive scaffold that transfers across AI tools and model generations, so their fluency does not expire when the tools change.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this framework when the goal is lasting capability rather than tool-specific instruction — when you want a learner who can work effectively with any AI tool they encounter in the future, not just the one they trained on today. It is the right foundation for organisations that want to build their own internal courses and need a structured, open-licensed framework to build on.

inputs.name: Learner with basic AI exposure
inputs.description: Someone who has done at least one hands-on AI task and is ready to move from trial-and-error to a structured approach. The 4D framework is more useful after a first experience than before it.

inputs.name: Domain-specific worked examples
inputs.description: Examples drawn from the learner's own industry or role that illustrate each of the four Ds in a context they recognise. The framework provides the structure; the examples provide the meaning.

inputs.name: A real problem to work on
inputs.description: A task from the learner's actual job that can serve as the vehicle for practising all four Ds in sequence — the raw material for the Masterpiece.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A person who can name and apply all four Ds to any new AI tool or task they encounter — not just the tools and tasks covered in the course.

outputs.name: Four-D self-assessment
outputs.description: A before-and-after self-assessment showing the learner's growth across each of the four dimensions — evidence that the framework has been internalised.

outputs.name: All-four-D task completion
outputs.description: A real work output produced by the learner using all four Ds in sequence: they chose what to delegate, wrote a clear description, evaluated the output with discernment, and checked for responsible use. This is the Masterpiece.

steps.name: Delegation — decide what to give to AI
steps.description: The learner maps their regular tasks against a simple test: is this task well-defined enough for AI to handle, repeatable enough to be worth automating, and low-enough-risk that an AI error is recoverable? Tasks that pass all three go on the delegation list. Tasks that fail any one stay human. The learner practices with ten tasks from their own role.

steps.name: Description — write a request that works
steps.description: The learner practises writing prompts that specify context, goal, format, and constraints — not just the task. They run a paired exercise: write a vague prompt, see the output, write a precise prompt, compare outputs. The pattern to internalise: more context produces better results, but the right context matters more than the most context.

steps.name: Discernment — evaluate what came back
steps.description: The learner practises reading AI output critically: is it accurate, is it complete, does it match the intent, could it mislead? They are given three AI outputs — one good, one plausible but wrong, one clearly wrong — and must identify which is which and explain why. The goal is a reliable inner critic, not distrust of all AI output.

steps.name: Diligence — use AI safely and ethically
steps.description: The learner works through the responsible-use questions for their own role. What data must not go into the AI? Who is accountable for AI-assisted work, and what attribution is required? What would a regulator or auditor ask about AI use in this context? Each question is answered for their specific job, not in the abstract.

principles: A scaffold that outlives any tool is worth more than tool-specific instructions that expire with the next model version.
All four Ds must be taught in sequence — each one depends on the one before it.
Domain examples are not decoration — a 4D course without role-specific examples is an abstract exercise, not a fluency programme.
patterns.threshold: The learner can apply all four Ds to a new AI task they have never seen before — choosing what to delegate, writing a precise description, evaluating the output critically, and identifying one responsible-use consideration — without prompting from an instructor.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed real work task where the learner has documented all four Ds in practice: their delegation decision, the description they used, their discernment evaluation of the AI output, and the diligence check they applied before using the result at work.

known_uses.name: Anthropic AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations
known_uses.quote: I want to prepare people for the long term. Not just how to use our latest features, but how to think about and work with AI—now and into the future.
known_uses.note: Developed with Prof. Joseph Feller (University College Cork) and Prof. Rick Dakan (Ringling College). Courses available for students, educators, and the general workforce. Free, with certificate of completion.

known_uses.name: Anthropic AI Fluency for Educators
known_uses.quote: empowers faculty, instructional designers, and educational leaders to apply AI Fluency into their own teaching practice and institutional strategy
known_uses.note: Demonstrates how the 4D framework is adapted for the educator persona — showing the persona-specific variant of this move.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of teaching the 4D framework as a concept without the paired hands-on exercises. A learner who can recite Delegation-Description-Discernment-Diligence but cannot apply each D to a real task has memorised a label, not built a skill.

The anti-pattern of running only the Delegation and Description modules because they feel more immediately useful, and treating Discernment and Diligence as supplementary. Without Discernment, learners pass bad AI outputs to colleagues. Without Diligence, they expose the organisation to data, copyright, or compliance risk.

sources.title: AI Fluency: Framework & Foundations
sources.quote: The framework consists of four pillars: Delegation, Description, Discernment, Diligence

sources.title: Anthropic's AI Fluency Course: How to Upskill Your Org with the 4D Framework
sources.quote: Margaret Vo, Head of Education at Anthropic, stated: 'I want to prepare people for the long term. Not just how to use our latest features, but how to think about and work with AI'

flowchart TD
    A[Learner with
basic AI exposure
+ real work problem] --> B[D1 Delegation
map tasks:
what to give AI]
    B --> C[D2 Description
practise precise
prompt writing]
    C --> D[D3 Discernment
evaluate output
critically]
    D --> E[D4 Diligence
responsible-use
checks for this role]
    E --> F[Before + after
self-assessment]
    F --> G{All four Ds

real task?}

    G -- Yes --> H[All-four-D task
completion
Masterpiece]

patterns.example_scenario: A senior procurement manager at a healthcare group is asked to pilot Anthropic's AI Fluency course for her 12-person team. She completes it herself first over two evenings. In the Delegation module she maps her weekly tasks and identifies three that pass all three delegation tests: drafting supplier evaluation summaries, checking contract terms against a standard clause library, and formatting cost comparison tables. Her daily tender review and final supplier selection fail the test — too much contextual judgment required. In the Description module she runs the paired exercise. First she asks the AI 'summarise this supplier response' (vague). Then she asks 'summarise this supplier response in three bullet points comparing price, delivery time, and compliance — flag any clause that deviates from our standard terms' (precise). The output difference is striking. She screenshots both for the team session. In the Discernment module she encounters the plausible-but-wrong example — a cost comparison the AI produced that transposed two figures — and realises she would not have caught it on a quick read. She updates her workflow to always print AI cost tables and trace-check numbers manually. In the Diligence module she identifies two data risks: patient data cannot go into the AI even in anonymised contract examples, and all AI-assisted tender documents must be attributed in the procurement log. She runs the team session using her own examples throughout. All twelve team members complete the framework course within two weeks. The masterpiece she presents to her director: a documented procurement AI workflow covering all four Ds, her own worked example for each, and a team attestation that all 12 have completed the Diligence check for their role.

patterns.name: Responsible-Use Guardrail
patterns.summary: Every training track at every Step includes an explicit module on responsible use: what not to do, what to check, and what to report. This guardrail runs cross-cutting through the Craft Path so safety norms are instilled before bad habits form. The pattern is not a single course; it is a structural commitment to embed responsible-use content at the entry point of every step of training. Without this, responsible-use rules are taught once at foundation level and then forgotten as learners advance to more capable tools. The Responsible-Use Guardrail treats safety as a recurring cost of advancement, not a one-time checkbox.
patterns.intent: Make responsible AI use a non-skippable condition of advancing to each new level of AI capability, so safety norms grow with the learner's power.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this pattern whenever designing a multi-step or multi-module AI training programme. It is a structural requirement, not an optional add-on. The guardrail must appear at the start of every Step — Foundation, Operator, and any Craft or Builder track — not only at the beginning of the whole programme. Also apply it as a corrective when an existing programme has responsible-use content only at the start.

inputs.name: Organisation AI use policy
inputs.description: A written, published policy stating what employees may and may not do with AI tools, covering data privacy, IP and copyright, hallucination risk, misuse detection, and escalation paths. The policy must exist before the guardrail module is written — the module is how the policy is taught, not a substitute for the policy.

inputs.name: Real incident examples
inputs.description: Anonymised examples of responsible-use failures from inside the organisation or from comparable organisations — not invented scenarios. Real examples carry more weight than hypotheticals.

inputs.name: Step-specific risk context
inputs.description: For each training step, the specific risks that learners at that level face — a foundation learner's risks (data privacy, sharing outputs carelessly) differ from an operator-level learner's risks (automating decisions, using AI in client-facing work).

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who, at each new level of AI capability, understands the specific responsible-use risks that level introduces and has acknowledged the rules that govern their expanded use.

outputs.name: Timestamped responsible-use acknowledgement per step
outputs.description: A documented, per-learner record that they have completed the responsible-use module at each step and acknowledged the policy — the Masterpiece of this cross-cutting pattern, because it creates a complete responsibility chain as capability grows.

outputs.name: Reduced incident rate
outputs.description: A measurable decrease in accidental data leaks, copyright breaches, and misuse reports after the guardrail programme is in place across all training steps.

steps.name: Define the policy before writing the module
steps.description: The responsible-use module is only as good as the policy it teaches. Before authoring any guardrail content, the organisation must have a written AI use policy that covers at minimum: what data cannot enter the AI, who is accountable for AI-assisted outputs, what constitutes misuse, and how to report a concern. If the policy does not exist, it must be written first.

steps.name: Cover the mandatory five areas at every step
steps.description: Each guardrail module, at every step, must cover five areas. The first two are hallucination risk (what the AI can get wrong and how to check) and data privacy (what cannot go in). The next two are IP and copyright (what cannot come out without attribution or review) and misuse detection (what misuse looks like at this capability level). The fifth is escalation (how to report a concern). The coverage deepens at each step to match the expanded capability.

steps.name: Use real incident examples, not hypotheticals
steps.description: Identify at least two real examples from inside the organisation or from published AI incident reports that illustrate responsible-use failures at the current level. Present them as case studies with outcomes. Anonymise internal examples. Hypotheticals are skipped; real cases are remembered.

steps.name: Require acknowledgement, not just completion
steps.description: At the end of each guardrail module, the learner must explicitly state that they have read and understood the policy and the step-specific risks — not just click through a completion screen. This acknowledgement is timestamped and stored. The combination of completion record and explicit acknowledgement is what produces defensible documentation.

principles: Responsible use is a recurring cost, not a one-time tax — it must appear at every step because power grows at every step.
Policy first, module second — no guardrail content before a written policy exists.
Acknowledgement is not completion — a learner who finishes the module but does not confirm understanding has not met the guardrail standard.
patterns.threshold: Every learner advancing to each new step has a timestamped acknowledgement of the step-specific responsible-use module on file, and the module covers all five mandatory areas for that step level.
patterns.masterpiece: A complete responsibility chain: for every learner in the organisation, a set of timestamped responsible-use acknowledgements — one per training step completed — that together demonstrate growing, documented AI safety awareness matching the learner's growing AI capability. Produceable as a single export for a regulatory or audit request.

known_uses.name: MasterCard responsible AI principles training
known_uses.quote: Eight hours of video training on responsible AI principles (fairness, transparency)
known_uses.note: Delivered as dedicated company-wide track, not appended to a tool tutorial.

known_uses.name: AWS Certified AI Practitioner — Domain 4: Guidelines for Responsible AI
known_uses.quote: Domain 4: Guidelines for Responsible AI (14% of scored content)
known_uses.note: Responsible use is a graded domain in the foundational AWS AI cert, not an elective.

known_uses.name: Google AI Essentials — Module 4: Use AI Responsibly
known_uses.quote: 5 modules: Introduction to AI, Maximize Productivity With AI Tools, Discover the Art of Prompting, Use AI Responsibly, Stay Ahead of the AI Curve
known_uses.note: Responsible use is the fourth of five modules — not an afterthought.

known_uses.name: EU AI Act Article 4 — role-specific literacy including risk
known_uses.quote: taking into account their technical knowledge, experience, education and training and the context the AI systems are to be used in, and considering the persons or groups of persons on whom the AI systems are to be used
known_uses.note: Article 4 requires risk-context awareness, making responsible-use a legally-grounded component of operator literacy.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of teaching responsible use only at foundation level and treating it as done. A learner who completes a foundation-level responsible-use module and then advances to operator-level automation tools faces new risks that were never covered. The guardrail must advance with the learner.

Recording module completion without a separate explicit acknowledgement step. In audit or incident investigation, completion alone does not demonstrate that the learner understood and accepted the rules. Acknowledgement is the legally and organisationally meaningful act.

sources.title: Article 4: AI literacy — EU Artificial Intelligence Act
sources.quote: taking into account the context the AI systems are to be used in

sources.title: Google AI Essentials — Use AI Responsibly (Module 4)
sources.quote: Use AI Responsibly

sources.title: AWS Certified AI Practitioner Exam Guide (AIF-C01)
sources.quote: Domain 4: Guidelines for Responsible AI (14% of scored content)

patterns.example_scenario: A global consulting firm rolls out AI tools in three waves: all staff get a chat AI in Q1, client-facing consultants get a document AI in Q2, and senior managers get an AI analytics tool in Q3. The L&D lead designs three guardrail modules — one per wave — each taking 30 minutes. The Q1 module covers the five base areas for a staff-level chat AI user. These are no client data in the chat window, no assuming the AI output is fact, no sharing outputs with clients without review, and how to report a concern. Every staff member completes it and acknowledges it on day one of chat AI access. The Q2 module covers the additional risks of the document AI: summarisation errors that could affect client deliverables, citation fabrication, and the need to attribute AI-assisted sections of reports. Every consultant completes it and acknowledges it before Q2 document AI access is granted. The Q3 module covers the analytics AI's specific risks: data bias in conclusions, presentation of AI-generated charts as primary analysis, and the regulatory considerations around AI-assisted advice in the firm's practice areas. Senior managers complete it before Q3 access. Six months after the programme is in place, the firm receives a client query about their AI use policies. The L&D lead exports the full responsibility chain — three acknowledgement records per person who holds all three access levels — in under 15 minutes. No incident during the six months required an escalation.
